question.text: Before the expiry of a futures contract, the intrinsic value of a Call Option on Futures is generally:

answer.text: Higher than that of an equivalent Call Option on Goods (because F > S before expiry)
Lower than Call Option on Goods
Equal to Call Option on Goods

explanation.text: **The Logic:** In normal conditions F > S (contango). For calls: IV = underlying - strike. Since F > S, Call on Futures IV (F-X) > Call on Goods IV (S-X). But for puts, the reverse applies. **The Trap:** Equal applies only at expiry when F converges to S. Lower is wrong for calls (it's correct for puts). The 'futures carry' component creates this difference. **Cross-Exam Context:** Comparing Options on Futures vs Options on Goods is specific to commodity markets. **Distributor's Pro-Tip:** This means a call Option on Futures is more expensive than equivalent Options on Goods — but this premium reflects the time value of money embedded in the futures price.
